standard_description: Fluently multiply and divide within 100, using strategies such as the relationship between multiplication and division (e.g., knowing that 8 × 5 = 40, one knows 40 ÷ 5 = 8) or properties of operations. By the end of Grade 3, know from memory all products of two one-digit numbers.
sources: Bay-Williams, J.M., & Kling, G. (2019). Math Fact Fluency: 60+ Games and Assessment Tools to Support Learning and Retention. ASCD.

Henry, V.J., & Brown, R.S. (2008). First-grade basic facts: An investigation into teaching and learning of an accelerated, high-demand memorization standard. Journal for Research in Mathematics Education, 39(2), 153–183.
NCTM Progressions for the Common Core State Standards in Mathematics — Operations and Algebraic Thinking (OA) K–5, 2011
Van de Walle, J.A., Karp, K.S., & Bay-Williams, J.M. (2013). Elementary and Middle School Mathematics (8th ed.). Pearson.
EngageNY Grade 3 Module 3 Topic A–G — Multiplication and Division with Units of 0, 1, 6–9, and Multiples of 10, 2013
Smarter Balanced Assessment Consortium — Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications, 2015

misconceptions.short_label: Adds instead of doubles for ×2 facts
misconceptions.description: For ×2 facts (the doubles), student adds 2 to the other factor instead of doubling it. Treats '×2' as '+2'. Most common in early multiplication when the link to addition is fresh but the meaning of × is fragile.
misconceptions.example: 7 × 2 = ? Student answers 9 (computes 7+2) instead of 14 (computes 7+7 or 2 sevens).
misconceptions.citation: Bay-Williams & Kling (2019) p.47 — 'The most persistent ×2 error is additive: students substitute the operation symbol with addition, producing answers exactly factor+2 too small. Doubling fluency requires explicit linking of ×2 to a known double fact.'
misconceptions.remediation: Anchor ×2 to doubles: '6×2 = 6+6 = 12.' Build a doubles chart from addition (5+5=10, 6+6=12, 7+7=14) and label each row '×2 fact.' Practice: 'Whenever you see ×2, say it out loud as: the number plus itself.'

misconceptions.description: Student uses skip counting as the primary strategy but loses track of how many skips they've made, especially for ×3, ×6, ×7 where intermediate values are hard to recognize. Often stops one skip early or one too late.
misconceptions.example: 6 × 7 = ? Student skip counts by 7: '7, 14, 21, 28, 35, 42' but reports the count after 5 skips → answers 35 instead of 42.
misconceptions.citation: Henry & Brown (2008) p.166 — 'Skip-count fluency erodes for non-friendly multiples; students lacking a tracking strategy (fingers, tally marks) consistently miscount on the 6th and 7th iterations.'
misconceptions.source: Henry & Brown, Journal for Research in Mathematics Education
misconceptions.remediation: Use finger tracking: one finger raised per skip. The number of fingers up must equal the multiplier. For 6×7, raise 6 fingers while saying 7, 14, 21, 28, 35, 42. Practice with a stop-when-fingers-match rule.

misconceptions.short_label: Thinks all ×5 products end in 5 (or 0) but applies wrong digit
misconceptions.description: Student knows ×5 products end in 0 or 5 but cannot reliably pick which one. May apply '×5 ends in 5' to even × 5 (which ends in 0) or vice versa. The pattern is half-internalized.
misconceptions.example: 6 × 5 = ? Student says 35 (wrong digit pattern; correct answer 30). Or 7 × 5 = 30 (also wrong; correct answer 35).
misconceptions.citation: Van de Walle et al. (2013) p.180 — 'The ×5 ending-digit pattern (0 for even × 5, 5 for odd × 5) is robust but requires explicit instruction; partial internalization yields half-correct products with the right "ending digit" but the wrong tens digit.'
misconceptions.source: Van de Walle Elementary and Middle School Mathematics
misconceptions.remediation: Teach the rule explicitly: 'Even × 5 ends in 0. Odd × 5 ends in 5.' Then handle the tens digit by halving: 'Take the other factor, half it, that's the tens (or tens+ones digit if odd).' E.g., 6 × 5: half of 6 is 3, ends in 0 → 30.

misconceptions.short_label: Doesn't use the digits-sum-to-9 pattern for ×9
misconceptions.description: For ×9 facts, student ignores the powerful pattern that the digits of the product sum to 9 (e.g., 9×4=36; 3+6=9). Instead, student guesses or skip counts laboriously, producing frequent errors. Also: tens digit = (other factor − 1).
misconceptions.example: 9 × 7 = ? Student guesses 64 or 65 (neither digits sum to 9). Correct: 63 (6+3=9, tens digit 6=7−1).
misconceptions.citation: Bay-Williams & Kling (2019) p.78 — 'The ×9 pattern (tens = factor−1, digits sum to 9) is the most powerful single-table shortcut but is rarely used by students who memorize each fact independently; explicit pattern instruction transforms ×9 fluency.'
misconceptions.remediation: Teach the two-rule pattern: '9 × n: tens digit is (n−1); ones digit is (10−n) so digits sum to 9.' Practice on a 9-table: 9×3=27 (2+7=9), 9×4=36 (3+6=9), 9×5=45 (4+5=9). Or use the finger trick (bend the n-th finger).

misconceptions.description: ×7 is the toughest single-digit fact family because it has no friendly pattern (unlike ×2/×5/×9). Student either guesses, skip counts (slowly, with errors), or refuses to attempt. Has not yet built ×7 from known facts via decomposition.
misconceptions.example: 7 × 8 = ? Student says 'I don't know' or guesses 54, 58, or 60. Correct: 56. Has not used 7 × 8 = (5 × 8) + (2 × 8) = 40 + 16.
misconceptions.citation: EngageNY G3 Module 3 Topic E Teacher Edition — 'The ×7 family is identified as the most difficult to memorize; instruction must explicitly provide decomposition strategies (5+2, 6+1, 8−1) to scaffold fluency rather than relying on rote.'
misconceptions.remediation: Teach ×7 = ×5 + ×2 decomposition: 7 × 8 = (5 × 8) + (2 × 8) = 40 + 16 = 56. Practice the 'split into 5 and 2' habit. Also teach commutative swap: if you know 8 × 7, swap to 7 × 8 — same answer.

misconceptions.description: For ×8 facts, student does not use the powerful chain ×8 = ×2 × ×2 × ×2 (triple doubling). Instead, slow skip counts by 8 with frequent errors. Has not yet linked ×8 to known doubles.
misconceptions.example: 8 × 6 = ? Student skip counts '8, 16, 24, 32, 40, 48' losing track at 4-th step → answers 40 or 56. Correct: 48. Could have used 6 × 8 = 6 → 12 → 24 → 48 (double three times).
misconceptions.citation: Bay-Williams & Kling (2019) p.74 — 'The ×8 family responds best to the triple-double strategy; students who memorize ×8 facts in isolation are prone to errors, while those who chain doubling stay accurate even at high products.'
misconceptions.remediation: Teach: '×8 = double, double, double the other factor.' Example for 6 × 8: 6 → 12 → 24 → 48. Three steps, all easy doubles. Practice the 'three jumps' visualization with arrows.

misconceptions.description: Student recalls a near-but-wrong fact, especially for facts they're learning. Confuses 4 × 6 = 24 with 4 × 7 = 28, often answering with the adjacent product. Suggests memorization without understanding.
misconceptions.example: 4 × 7 = ? Student answers 24 (which is 4 × 6) or 32 (which is 4 × 8). Cannot distinguish neighboring products in the table.
misconceptions.citation: Henry & Brown (2008) p.171 — 'Errors near the target fact are characteristic of partial memorization without conceptual anchoring; students confuse n × k with n × (k±1) because the products differ by only n, which is a small perturbation in working memory.'
misconceptions.source: Henry & Brown, Journal for Research in Mathematics Education
misconceptions.remediation: Anchor each fact to a model (array, equation, story). For 4 × 7, draw 4 rows of 7 dots and count by 7s. Compare to 4 × 6 visually — 4 × 7 has one MORE column. Practice 'one more group' reasoning: 4 × 7 = 4 × 6 + 4 = 24 + 4 = 28.
